hook: You know how to diagonalize a square matrix $A$ as $A = PDP^{-1}$ when it has enough eigenvectors, and you've seen that symmetric matrices always diagonalize nicely as $A = Q\Lambda Q^T$ with orthonormal columns. But what do you do when $A$ is not square — say a $3 \times 2$ matrix — or when $A$ is square but not symmetric? Eigendecomposition simply breaks down. Can we still find some kind of 'diagonal' factorization that works for ANY matrix?
concept: The Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) factorizes any $m \times n$ matrix $A$ into three matrices: $A = U\Sigma V^T$, where $U$ is $m \times m$ orthogonal, $V$ is $n \times n$ orthogonal, and $\Sigma$ is $m \times n$ with non-negative entries $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \cdots \geq 0$ on its main diagonal and zeros elsewhere. The diagonal entries $\sigma_i$ are called singular values. Every real matrix, regardless of shape or rank, has an SVD.
anatomy: The SVD $A = U\Sigma V^T$ has three distinct structural parts. The matrix $V$ has columns $v_1, \ldots, v_n$ called the right singular vectors — they are an orthonormal basis for $\mathbb{R}^n$ (the domain), found as eigenvectors of $A^T A$. The matrix $U$ has columns $u_1, \ldots, u_m$ called the left singular vectors — they form an orthonormal basis for $\mathbb{R}^m$ (the codomain), found as eigenvectors of $AA^T$. The matrix $\Sigma$ is a generalized diagonal whose non-zero diagonal entries $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i}$ are the singular values, where $\lambda_i$ are the positive eigenvalues of $A^T A$ (equivalently of $AA^T$). The rank of $A$ equals the number of non-zero singular values.

worked_example.description_latex: We want to find the SVD of this $3 \times 2$ matrix $A$. Because it is already in a near-diagonal form, this example will let us see the structure of $U$, $\Sigma$, and $V$ clearly without heavy computation.

We compute $A^T A$ first because its eigenvectors give the right singular vectors (the columns of $V$). Here $A^T A$ is already diagonal, so its eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = 9$ and $\lambda_2 = 4$, with eigenvectors $v_1 = e_1$ and $v_2 = e_2$. This means $V = I_2$.

The singular values are the square roots of the eigenvalues of $A^T A$, listed in decreasing order. Notice that $\Sigma$ has the same shape as $A$ itself — it is $3 \times 2$ — and the zeros in the third row reflect the fact that $A$ has rank 2, not 3.

worked_example.expression_latex: u_1 = \frac{1}{\sigma_1} A v_1 = \frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix}3\\0\\0\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}1\\0\\0\end{pmatrix}, \quad u_2 = \frac{1}{\sigma_2} A v_2 = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix}0\\2\\0\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}0\\1\\0\end{pmatrix}
worked_example.description_latex: We build the left singular vectors using the relation $u_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i} A v_i$, which guarantees $A v_i = \sigma_i u_i$ — the geometric heart of the SVD. Because $A$ is $3 \times 2$, we need a third column $u_3$ to complete $U$; we choose any unit vector orthogonal to $u_1$ and $u_2$, so $u_3 = e_3$.

Assembling the three factors confirms $A = U\Sigma V^T$: multiplying out returns the original matrix $A$. In this simple case $U = I_3$ and $V = I_2$, which makes sense because $A$ was already 'aligned' with the standard basis axes.

partial_example.description_latex: We start by computing $A^T A$ to find the right singular vectors. The matrix $A^T A$ has eigenvalues $\lambda_1 = 2$ and $\lambda_2 = 0$, which we will use next.

Here are the normalized eigenvectors of $A^T A$. Your job now is to find the singular values $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$, construct $\Sigma$ (remember $A$ is $2 \times 2$), and then compute the left singular vector $u_1 = \frac{1}{\sigma_1}Av_1$.

Because $\sigma_2 = 0$, we cannot use $u_2 = \frac{1}{\sigma_2}Av_2$; instead we choose $u_2$ to be any unit vector orthogonal to $u_1$. Now write out the full factorization $A = U\Sigma V^T$ with explicit matrices and verify it recovers $A$.

practice_problems.answer_latex: U = \begin{pmatrix}1&0&0\\0&1&0\\0&0&1\end{pmatrix},\; \Sigma = \begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix},\; V = \begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}, \text{ so } A = U\Sigma V^T

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute $A^T A$, find its eigenvalues, and state the singular values of $A$.

Using the SVD $A = U\Sigma V^T$, express the rank of $A$ and find a basis for $\text{Null}(A)$ in terms of the columns of $V$, given that $A$ is $3 \times 3$ with singular values $\sigma_1 = 5, \sigma_2 = 2, \sigma_3 = 0$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{rank}(A) = 2; \; \text{Null}(A) = \text{span}\{v_3\}, \text{ where } v_3 \text{ is the third column of } V

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A data scientist says the 'best rank-1 approximation' of a matrix $A$ with SVD $A = U\Sigma V^T$ is $A_1 = \sigma_1 u_1 v_1^T$. For $A = \begin{pmatrix}3&0\\0&2\end{pmatrix}$ with $\sigma_1=3$, $u_1=e_1$, $v_1=e_1$, compute $A_1$ and find $\|A - A_1\|_F$ (the Frobenius norm of the error).

common_mistakes: Students often compute $A^T A$ and $AA^T$ independently but then pair their eigenvectors arbitrarily, producing columns of $U$ and $V$ that are inconsistent with the relation $Av_i = \sigma_i u_i$. Always derive $u_i$ from $u_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i}Av_i$ to ensure the signs and ordering are consistent.

When a singular value $\sigma_k = 0$, students try to compute $u_k = \frac{1}{0}Av_k$ and get confused. For zero singular values, $u_k$ must be chosen as a unit vector orthogonal to all previously found $u_i$ — it cannot be derived from the formula, and any valid orthonormal completion of the set works.

Students frequently confuse the shape of $\Sigma$: they make it square $\min(m,n) \times \min(m,n)$ instead of the same shape $m \times n$ as $A$. Remember that $\Sigma$ must match $A$'s dimensions so that the product $U\Sigma V^T$ is $m \times m$ times $m \times n$ times $n \times n$, giving an $m \times n$ result.
untested_variants: Computing the SVD of a complex matrix $A \in \mathbb{C}^{m \times n}$, where the factorization becomes $A = U\Sigma V^*$ with unitary (not merely orthogonal) $U$ and $V$.
Using the SVD to compute the pseudoinverse $A^+ = V\Sigma^+ U^T$ and solving least-squares problems $\min \|Ax - b\|$.

Proving or applying the Eckart–Young theorem — that the best rank-$k$ approximation in Frobenius or spectral norm is $A_k = \sum_{i=1}^k \sigma_i u_i v_i^T$ — in an optimization or data-compression context.

Relating the SVD to the geometry of linear maps: interpreting $V^T$ as a rotation/reflection in the domain, $\Sigma$ as stretching along coordinate axes, and $U$ as a rotation/reflection in the codomain.
